front: Short Story: the protagonist stammers "Isn't life-" to her brother Laurie after delivering leftover food to her dead neighbor's family
Short Story: Jose plays "This Life is Weary" on the piano while the maid Sadie requests sandwich flags
Short Story: Godber's delivery man brings cream puffs and news that the carter Mr. Scott was killed by his horse
Short Story: opens with "And after all the weather was ideal"; the protagonist directs the placement of a marquee "bang slap in the eye"
Short Story: the protagonist apologizes for her black hat trimmed with gold daisies after seeing a "wonderful, beautiful" corpse "given up to his dream"
Short Story: the protagonist holds a piece of bread and butter while supervising workmen hanging a marquee, then eventually takes a big bite
Short Story: the band wears green coats and is described as "too like frogs for words" by Kitty Maitland
Short Story: the protagonist condemns the class distinction between herself and a workman who smells a sprig of lavender
Short Story: Bertha Young discovers her husband Harry is having an affair with Pearl Fulton after they had stood together gazing at a pear tree
Short Story: a pear tree stretches up "like the flame of a candle"; at the end it is "as lovely as ever and as full of flower"
Short Story: the poet Eddie Warren praises a poem beginning "Why must it always be tomato soup?" and declares "nothing so eternal as tomato soup"
Short Story: two women stand "caught in that circle of unearthly light, understanding each other perfectly" with "silver flowers" dropping from their hair
Short Story: the protagonist keeps her excitement hidden "like a rare, rare fiddle" and insists on feeding her baby Little B herself
Short Story: guests include the Norman Knights (one wears an orange coat with monkeys) and a playwright who complains about a speeding taxi driver
Short Story: a young couple on a park bench insults the protagonist's fur, comparing it to a "fried whiting"
Short Story: at the end, the lonely protagonist thinks "she heard something crying" after putting away her beloved fur
Short Story: an English teacher at the Jardins Publiques censors her own thoughts, describing her breathing as "light and sad" then correcting to "gentle"
Short Story: a man rejects an "ermine toque" before young lovers ask "why doesn't she keep her silly old mug at home?"
Short Story: sisters Constantia and Josephine realize they no longer need to silence the barrel organ that always annoyed their dead father
back: "The Daughters of the Late Colonel" (Katherine Mansfield)
work: The Daughters of the Late Colonel

front: Short Story: Nurse Andrews takes excessive helpings of bread and butter while Con and Jug cope with their father's death
back: "The Daughters of the Late Colonel" (Katherine Mansfield)
work: The Daughters of the Late Colonel

front: Short Story: sisters discuss giving a watch to nephew Cyril, who is asked if his father is fond of meringue, and debate firing the maid Kate
back: "The Daughters of the Late Colonel" (Katherine Mansfield)
work: The Daughters of the Late Colonel

front: Short Story: Else whispers "I seen the little lamp" after Kezia lets the shunned Kelvey children see the title object in her backyard
Short Story: the Kelvey children are shunned because their father is in prison; they wear clothes made from tablecloths and curtains
Short Story: "the boss" drops three blobs of ink on the title insect, killing it, after being reminded of his son's death in Belgium during WWI by a visit from Woodifield
Short Story: a character chops off a duck's head with a tomahawk to prove it can walk headless
work: Prelude / The Aloe / At the Bay

front: Short Story: Linda Fairfield realizes life is "laughable, simply laughable" while observing an aloe in her garden
work: Prelude / The Aloe / At the Bay

front: Short Story: Beryl Fairfield rejects the advances of a drunken Harry Kember at the end of this story set over one day
work: Prelude / The Aloe / At the Bay

front: Short Story: a girl is taken by Maori women to the beach and has an enjoyable day before "little blue men" carry her back to the "House of Boxes"
Author: inspired the character Gudrun in Women in Love; possibly caught fatal tuberculosis from D.H. Lawrence
Author: husband was John Middleton Murry, who published her work in journals like Adelphi
Author: joined George Gurdjieff's Institute for the Harmonious Development of Man shortly after publishing The Garden Party and Other Stories
Author: Claire Harman's biography All Sorts of Lives defends this author against charges of plagiarizing Chekhov's "Sleepy" during a hospital stay in Bad Worishofen
Author: described her stories as a "cry against corruption" in a letter to her husband
Short Story: a lonely woman points to a "big nail to the right of the front door" that once held the cage of a bird bought from a Chinese man
Short Story: "Roo-coo-coo-coo" prompts Reggie to wonder if he and Anne could be like the two title birds
Short Story: a boy bursts into tears when his sister takes a nut that was a doorknob off an ice pudding
Short Story: Grandma explains death was "God's will" on the boat to Picton with her granddaughter Fenella
Short Story: a girl dances with young men who remark on the floor quality before an old man upsets her by saying no one will kiss her when she's old
Short Story: the title character realizes "there was nowhere" for her to cry after leaving a "literary gentleman's" flat to mourn her grandson's death
Short Story: Isabel feels guilt after reading friends a passionate love letter from her husband William
